title: Comply: Learning Sentences with Complex Weights inspired by Fruit Fly Olfaction
authors: Alexei Figueroa1Justus Westerhoff1‚ôĘGolzar Atefi1‚ôĘDennis Fast1Benjamin Winter2Felix Alexader Gers1Alexander L√∂ser1Wolfang Nejdl31DATEXIS, Berliner Hochschule f√ľr Technik (BHT)2Medical AI Analytics & Information GmbH3L3S, Leibniz University Hannover{afigueroa,justus.westerhoff}@bht-berlin.de‚ôĘ\diamondsuitAuthors contributed equallyWork done at BHT.
abstract: Biologically inspired neural networks offer alternative avenues to model data distributions. FlyVec is a recent example that draws inspiration from the fruit fly‚Äôs olfactory circuit to tackle the task of learning word embeddings. Surprisingly, this model performs competitively even against deep learning approaches specifically designed to encode text, and it does so with the highest degree of computational efficiency. We pose the question of whether this performance can be improved further. For this, we introduce Comply. By incorporating positional information through complex weights, we enable a single-layer neural network to learn sequence representations. Our experiments show that Comply not only supersedes FlyVec but also performs on par with significantly larger state-of-the-art models. We achieve this without additional parameters. Comply yields sparse contextual representations of sentences that can be interpreted explicitly from the neuron weights.

paragraphs: A large variety of deep learning models have centered on creating distributional language representations in recent years. Among these, Transformer-based architectures such as BERT [Kenton and Toutanova, 2019] have consistently set the state-of-the-art and are now considered baselines for Natural Language Processing (NLP) tasks. However, better performance in metrics has come at the expense of a seemingly ever-growing computational complexity. Naturally, there is a competing interest in developing efficient architectures, and often, these are inspired by intelligent biological systems that are efficient by nature. Recently, FlyVec was proposed as a simple approach for creating contextual word embeddings motivated by the olfactory circuit of the fruit fly [Yuchen et al., 2021]. These embeddings are binary vectors (hash codes) that are formed through neural inhibition via the kk-Winner-Takes-All (kk-WTA) activation. While FlyVec is competitive with traditional word embedding methods like word2vec [Mikolov et al., 2013] and GloVe [Pennington et al., 2014], it is still superseded by contextual embedding methods such as BERT [Kenton and Toutanova, 2019]. Although FlyVec demonstrates an elegant approach that is more computationally efficient, it motivates the question of whether the performance can still be improved, while maintaining the same scale and architectural simplicity.

We attempt to tackle this by, yet again, gathering inspiration from how fruit flies learn to identify odors. Previous research has focused on the spatial bundling and modularity of neurons to create high-dimensional representations. However, olfactory processing also involves processing temporal patterns, e.g., firing delays and duration. We highlight two ways in which fruit flies utilize temporal information.

Relevance of odor arrival time: The way fruit flies perceive a mixture of odors hints at how the arrival order of stimuli leads to better segregation and classification capabilities. Multiple odors can be emitted either from the same source or from different sources. Odors emitted from a single source have an approximately simultaneous arrival time due to the physical process of turbulent diffusion, as opposed to odors propagating from multiple sources, which results in distinct arrival times. Several studies have shown that fruit flies [Szyszka et al., 2012, Hopfield, 1991, Sehdev et al., 2019], as well as other insects [Baker et al., 1998, Saha et al., 2013, Nikonov and Leal, 2002, Witzgall and Priesner, 1991, Andersson et al., 2011], likely use this asynchronous arrival time to perceptually segregate mixed odors.

Using time as a medium to encode odors: Olfactory receptor neurons (ORNs) respond in a temporally heterogeneous manner that depends on both the type of odor and the type of ORNs [Raman et al., 2010]. This heterogeneity encompasses the firing rate and the time course (latency and duration), which results in odor identification irrespective of stimulus concentration [Stopfer et al., 2003]. Mutant flies that are ablated of spatial coding retain odor discriminatory capacity only through the temporal responses of ORNs [DasGupta and Waddell, 2008, Olsen et al., 2007].

These two perspectives regarding time illustrate how spatial encoding (in the sense of neuron collocation) is not the only process employed to generate odor embeddings. We investigate the impact of integrating this temporal information in the quality of the embeddings. Our method, Comply, generalizes FlyVec to incorporate time information (word positions) in the analogy of mirroring text sequences (sentences) to odor segregation. This is accomplished by expressing the input with phase differences and exploiting the semantics of complex numbers. Consequently, we learn a complex-valued parameter matrix through an extension of an unsupervised energy function. We subject our models to a comprehensive evaluation in semantic sentence similarity tasks, as well as a text-based Reinforcement Learning environment. Our experiments show how the features generated by Comply boost the performance significantly, while preserving the parameter complexity, computational efficiency, and biological plausibility of FlyVec. The main contributions of this work can be summarized as follows: We make the source code111https://github.com/DATEXIS/comply available to reproduce the results of our experiments.

‚ÄĘ We introduce a novel architecture that can produce sparse encodings (binary hashes) incorporating positional information, while preserving computational efficiency and significantly boosting performance.

‚ÄĘ Through a comprehensive quantitative evaluation of the unsupervised representations, we show the enhanced quality of the produced embeddings, being competitive with significantly larger architectures in NLP.
‚ÄĘ We showcase qualitatively that the simplicity of our approach, Comply, retains interpretable properties of FlyVec, while extending to the temporal dimension of sequences.

subsections.title: Bio hashing.
paragraphs: The mushroom body of the fruit fly is considered to be the center of olfactory learning and memory formation [de Belle and Heisenberg, 1994]. It is where projection neurons (PN) make synaptic connections with Kenyon cells (KC). In contrast to PNs, KCs have much more odor-selective responses [Turner et al., 2008, Honegger et al., 2011]. In other words, only a small proportion of them becomes activated for a given odor due to inhibition [Lin et al., 2014, Papadopoulou et al., 2011]. Therefore, the KCs represent odors as sparse and distributed hash codes [Lin et al., 2014]. These codes are high-dimensional representations that the mushroom body output neurons (MBONs) use to form memories. The sparsity and high specificity of these KC responses are thought to support the accuracy of this process[Laurent, 2002].

The FlyVec [Yuchen et al., 2021] model repurposes bio hashing into learning word embeddings from raw text. Using a one-layer neural network from PNs to KCs, it creates representations of a binarized input of context and target words. The synaptic weights from PNs to KCs are learned with a biologically plausible unsupervised learning algorithm [Krotov and Hopfield, 2019] inspired by Hebbian learning. During inference, the kk-Winner-Takes-All (kk-WTA) mechanism creates sparse, binary hash codes.

Our method, Comply, uses a similar architecture and algorithm. However, we augment the inputs with positional information and extend the loss and parameters accordingly. Specifically, Comply generalizes FlyVec, enabling bio hashes to represent sequences.

subsections.title: Text embeddings.
paragraphs: The main goal of embedding text is to produce vectors that have a semantic correspondence to characters, words, or arbitrary sequences of text elements (tokens). Examples are word2vec [Mikolov et al., 2013], GloVe (Global Vectors) [Pennington et al., 2014], and BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) [Kenton and Toutanova, 2019]. The former two are examples of static word embeddings, which are constant regardless of context. In contrast, BERT learns a function that can yield representations conditioned on the context: a contextual word embedding.

Transformer-based encoder models like BERT learn language representations using self-attention and masked language modeling among other objectives, which are not necessarily motivated by biological mechanisms. These representations are ‚Äúcontextual‚ÄĚ, i.e., they capture the ambiguity of words (sentences) given the context in which they appear.

The FlyVec model highlights how a biologically inspired architecture is also capable of learning contextual word embeddings (binary hash codes). In our work, we extend this model to sentence representations. We do this by learning positions as a complementary distributional property of text.

subsections.title: Positional encoding.
paragraphs: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) [Rumelhart et al., 1986], e.g., Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) [Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997, Gers et al., 2000], inherently capture positional information given that their state (and output) is always conditioned on the sequential input. Similarly, Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) can implicitly learn to incorporate positional information [Islam et al., 2020]. Although self-attention [Lin et al., 2017] is designed to focus on different parts of the sequence depending on the task, it does not infer positional information. When introducing Transformers, the work [Vaswani et al., 2017] explicitly incorporates positional encodings in an additive manner. In fact, this component is crucial for the success of these architectures [Sinha et al., 2021]. Closer to our work, RoPe [Su et al., 2024] expresses positional information in Transformer models through a multiplicative approach. Namely, an embedding vector of a token is rotated based on its position. Although this approach and ours are both non-parametric and leverage the complex plane to implement such rotations, we differ by explicitly expressing the input in the complex field and learning a complex parameter matrix.

subsections.title: Complex weights.
paragraphs: Complex-valued Neural Networks (CVNNs) offer advantages in specific domains, as they can handle both amplitudes and phases of the signals. Examples of such areas are image processing, and more generally signal processing where a datum is inherently complex. For a comprehensive overview see, e.g., [Lee et al., 2022]. Close to our work, the method in [Wang et al., 2020] uses a learned complex-valued map to enrich text embeddings with positional information. In our work, using the semantics of the complex field yields a simple mathematical extension of the FlyVec model. While CVNNs focus on end-to-end functions in the complex field, we use complex numbers only as a way of efficiently representing the order in sequences learned in neurons. However, we do not use complex activation functions, our embeddings are purely real and are constructed with the kk-WTA mechanism.

subsections.title: 3.1 FlyVec[Yuchen et¬†al., 2021].
paragraphs: This model adapts a single parameter matrix ūĚźĖ‚ąą‚ĄĚK√ó2‚ÄčNvoc\boldsymbol{\mathbf{W}}\in\mathbb{R}^{K\times 2N_{\text{voc}}}, modeling the PNs‚Üí\toKCs synapses (projection neurons to Kenyon cells) and using a biologically inspired mechanism. Here, KK is the number KCs, and NvocN_{\text{voc}} is the size of the word vocabulary. For training, every input sentence is split into sliding text windows of fixed length ŌČ‚ąą‚Ąē\omega\in\mathbb{N} disregarding positions, thus modeling sets (bag-of-words). The inputs ūĚźĮ‚ąą‚ĄĚ2‚ÄčNvoc\boldsymbol{\mathbf{v}}\in\mathbb{R}^{2N_{\text{voc}}} are crafted as follows: the first NvocN_{\text{voc}} components are the sum of one-hot encoded vectors of context tokens surrounding a target word, and the second NvocN_{\text{voc}} entries are a single one-hot vector corresponding to the target word, see fig.ňú1.
The parameter matrix ūĚźĖ\boldsymbol{\mathbf{W}} is learned by minimizing the energy function
where

ūĚźĮ/ūĚź©\boldsymbol{\mathbf{v}}/\boldsymbol{\mathbf{p}} is the input vector as described above divided by the word frequencies ūĚź©‚ąą‚ĄēNvoc\boldsymbol{\mathbf{p}}\in\mathbb{N}^{N_{\text{voc}}} in the whole training corpus, and D‚Äča‚Äčt‚ÄčaData is the set of preprocessed inputs. The model emphasizes learning through lateral inhibition, i.e., during training, there is only a single neuron őľ^\widehat{\mu} (a row ūĚźĖőľ^\boldsymbol{\mathbf{W}}_{\widehat{\mu}} in the ūĚźĖ\boldsymbol{\mathbf{W}} matrix), which is adapted for a single sample.

subsections.title: 3.2 Comply
paragraphs: Our extension revolvese around capturing the sequential nature of text data through added positional information. We leverage the semantics of complex numbers and keep the same biologically inspired mechanisms, namely sparsity through kk-Winner-Takes-All (kk-WTA) activations. We aim to preserve the properties of FlyVec regarding efficiency, simplicity, and interpretability. Thus, we constrain ourselves to a single-parameter matrix. Additionally, we focus on an extension that considers a complex-valued input, a single complex parameter matrix ūĚźĖ\boldsymbol{\mathbf{W}}, and a compatible energy function EE.

For each sentence expressed as a concatenation of one-hot encoded vectors, we multiply each vector by ei‚ÄčŌÄ‚ÄčlLe^{i\pi\frac{l}{L}}, where LL is the length of the sentence, l‚ąą{0,1,‚Ä¶,L‚ąí1}l\in\{0,1,\dotsc,L-1\} denotes the position of the respective word in the sentence and ii is the imaginary unit, see fig.ňú2.

We denote this preprocessed input by ūĚź≥‚ąą‚ĄāL√óNvoc\boldsymbol{\mathbf{z}}\in\mathbb{C}^{L\times N_{\text{voc}}} and its complex conjugate by ūĚź≥¬Į\overline{\boldsymbol{\mathbf{z}}}. Note that this modification, compared to FlyVec, expands the dimension of the input by LL, and reduces the length of each word vector by half since we do not have a target word as in fig.ňú1. Consequently, our parameter matrix ūĚźĖ\boldsymbol{\mathbf{W}} is in ‚ĄāK√óNvoc\mathbb{C}^{K\times N_{\text{voc}}} and has the same number of parameters as FlyVec (2‚ÄčK‚ÄčNvoc2KN_{\text{voc}}), since for each complex parameter two real numbers are required.
With this modified input, an immediate extension of eq.ňú1 with the Hermitian inner product ‚ü®‚čÖ,‚čÖ‚ü©H\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle_{H} is not directly applicable, since for a sentence the quantity

is complex and ‚Ąā\mathbb{C} is unordered, hence finding the argmax\operatorname{argmax} as in eq.ňú2 is not possible. Thus, we adapt EE to find a coherent maximally activated neuron őľ^\widehat{\mu}.

Due to the complex-valued nature of the Hermitian inner product, an alternative extension must be considered. If we disregard word repetition, we can think of eq.ňú3 as ‚ü®ūĚźĖőľ,ūĚź≥~‚ü©H\langle\boldsymbol{\mathbf{W}}_{\mu},\tilde{\boldsymbol{\mathbf{z}}}\rangle_{H}, where ūĚź≥~‚ČĒ‚ąĎl‚ąąLūĚź≥l\tilde{\boldsymbol{\mathbf{z}}}\coloneqq\sum_{l\in L}\boldsymbol{\mathbf{z}}_{l} is a multi-hot encoded version of the input (similar to FlyVec). We can decompose ‚ü®Wőľ,ūĚź≥~‚ü©H\langle W_{\mu},\tilde{\boldsymbol{\mathbf{z}}}\rangle_{H} into two steps, element-wise multiplication and aggregation, and yield real values that are proportional to neuron activation and can be sorted: Equationňú4 reflects these modifications to the energy function EE. Note that the energy of the original FlyVec model (see eq.ňú1) is a special case of our approach when ūĚźĖ\boldsymbol{\mathbf{W}} is real and the inputs are preprocessed as in fig.ňú1. We slightly abuse notation by writing LL and {1,‚Ä¶,L}\{1,\dotsc,L\} interchangeably, and define where Arg\operatorname{Arg} is the function that extracts the phases from the complex numbers, and sls_{l} is the index in the vocabulary of the llth word in a sentence [s0,‚Ä¶,sL‚ąí1][s_{0},...,s_{L-1}]. Equationňú4 favors learning complex weights for words in one half of the complex plane, since the absolute angle difference |Arg‚čÖ|\lvert\operatorname{Arg}\cdot\rvert is maximized, and its maximum is at ŌÄ\pi. If we replaced the additions in both eqs.ňú4 and 5 with subtractions, the synapses would be learned on the same half-plane as the input vectors ūĚź≥\boldsymbol{\mathbf{z}}. Either option is of no consequence for the model and we choose the additive expression for our experiments. Furthermore, we highlight that the two summands in eq.ňú5 are not of the same scale; the former, |‚ü®ūĚźĖőľ,ūĚź≥l‚ü©H|\lvert\langle\boldsymbol{\mathbf{W}}_{\mu},\boldsymbol{\mathbf{z}}_{l}\rangle_{H}\rvert, is in [0,‚ąě)[0,\infty), whereas the latter, |Arg‚ü®ūĚźĖőľ,ūĚź≥l‚ü©H|\lvert\operatorname{Arg}\langle\boldsymbol{\mathbf{W}}_{\mu},\boldsymbol{\mathbf{z}}_{l}\rangle_{H}\rvert, is in [0,ŌÄ][0,\pi]. To consider this, we experimented with replacing addition (++) by multiplication (‚čÖ\cdot) in eq.ňú4 and eq.ňú5. This however resulted in instability leading to model overfitting (multiple neurons learn the same sequences) and, more prominently, would not constitute a generalization of the original energy presented in eq.ňú1. Nevertheless, in the case of inference, we also test the multiplicative approach.

‚ÄĘ Element-wise multiplication: For every word and neuron, we perform a complex multiplication, which translates to scaling absolutes and subtracting phases. The former indicates the correlation between the input intensity and the neuron‚Äôs output, while the latter gives us a measure of the distance (temporal difference) between the word in neuron ūĚźĖőľ\boldsymbol{\mathbf{W}}_{\mu} and the sentence.

‚ÄĘ Aggregation: Simply adding these complex numbers does not retain positional difference information, since ei‚Äčőł=ei‚Äč2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äčőłe^{i\theta}=e^{i2\pi\theta} for any őł‚ąą‚ĄĚ\theta\in\mathbb{R}. Hence, we instead sum the magnitudes and absolute values of phase differences to capture both the effect of maximum word activation (as in the FlyVec model) and the word position distance.

Element-wise multiplication: For every word and neuron, we perform a complex multiplication, which translates to scaling absolutes and subtracting phases. The former indicates the correlation between the input intensity and the neuron‚Äôs output, while the latter gives us a measure of the distance (temporal difference) between the word in neuron ūĚźĖőľ\boldsymbol{\mathbf{W}}_{\mu} and the sentence.

Aggregation: Simply adding these complex numbers does not retain positional difference information, since ei‚Äčőł=ei‚Äč2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äčőłe^{i\theta}=e^{i2\pi\theta} for any őł‚ąą‚ĄĚ\theta\in\mathbb{R}. Hence, we instead sum the magnitudes and absolute values of phase differences to capture both the effect of maximum word activation (as in the FlyVec model) and the word position distance.

Note that, as depicted in fig.ňú2, we limit the phases in the input ūĚź≥\boldsymbol{\mathbf{z}} to lie in [0,ŌÄ)[0,\pi) to enforce learning phases in ūĚźĖ\boldsymbol{\mathbf{W}} that also only span half of the complex plane. This resolves the ambiguity of having distinct positions in word phases that would be mapped equivalently in the phase distance component of our energy function, e.g., a word appearing in ‚ąíŌÄ2\frac{-\pi}{2} and ŌÄ2\frac{\pi}{2} and the weight of that word in ūĚźĖőľ\boldsymbol{\mathbf{W}}_{\mu} with phase 0.
To summarize, the semantics and operations of complex numbers naturally give us a framework to generalize the objective of minimizing EE, while capturing positional information.

We illustrate the mechanics of the loss presented in eq.ňú4 with a toy example that uses only four KCs (K=4K=4) and ten words (digits) (Nvoc=10N_{\text{voc}}=10) for two sentences (sequences): 1,2,‚Ä¶,91,2,\dotsc,9 and 9,8,‚Ä¶,19,8,\dotsc,1. The model is trained with backpropagation following eq.ňú4. Figureňú3 shows the initial state in the complex plane for each neuron, as well as the converging state after learning. Both sequences are explicitly learned in the network in the opposite half-plane of the input (that is, with angles in [‚ąíŌÄ;0)[-\pi;0)). The 11-WTA activation (eq.ňú4) successfully creates a sparse representation of the two sequences without interference. This can be observed since the remaining neurons are unchanged and the two sequences are learned (imprinted) in two distinct neurons.

We construct binary feature vectors for Comply from the order of activations of the KCs. Specifically, as in [Ryali et al., 2020], the output vector ūĚź°‚ąą{0,1}K\boldsymbol{\mathbf{h}}\in\{0,1\}^{K} of an input ūĚź≥\boldsymbol{\mathbf{z}} is defined for all őľ‚ąą{1,‚Ä¶,K}\mu\in\{1,\dotsc,K\} as

where k‚ąą‚Ąēk\in\mathbb{N}, the hash length, is a hyperparameter. Since the two summands are, as noted earlier, of different scales, we also experiment with a version we call ComplyM that does multiplication (‚čÖ\cdot) instead of addition (++) in eq.ňú6 above.

paragraphs: For each sentence expressed as a concatenation of one-hot encoded vectors, we multiply each vector by ei‚ÄčŌÄ‚ÄčlLe^{i\pi\frac{l}{L}}, where LL is the length of the sentence, l‚ąą{0,1,‚Ä¶,L‚ąí1}l\in\{0,1,\dotsc,L-1\} denotes the position of the respective word in the sentence and ii is the imaginary unit, see fig.ňú2.

Note that this modification, compared to FlyVec, expands the dimension of the input by LL, and reduces the length of each word vector by half since we do not have a target word as in fig.ňú1. Consequently, our parameter matrix ūĚźĖ\boldsymbol{\mathbf{W}} is in ‚ĄāK√óNvoc\mathbb{C}^{K\times N_{\text{voc}}} and has the same number of parameters as FlyVec (2‚ÄčK‚ÄčNvoc2KN_{\text{voc}}), since for each complex parameter two real numbers are required.

is complex and ‚Ąā\mathbb{C} is unordered, hence finding the argmax\operatorname{argmax} as in eq.ňú2 is not possible. Thus, we adapt EE to find a coherent maximally activated neuron őľ^\widehat{\mu}.

paragraphs: Due to the complex-valued nature of the Hermitian inner product, an alternative extension must be considered. If we disregard word repetition, we can think of eq.ňú3 as ‚ü®ūĚźĖőľ,ūĚź≥~‚ü©H\langle\boldsymbol{\mathbf{W}}_{\mu},\tilde{\boldsymbol{\mathbf{z}}}\rangle_{H}, where ūĚź≥~‚ČĒ‚ąĎl‚ąąLūĚź≥l\tilde{\boldsymbol{\mathbf{z}}}\coloneqq\sum_{l\in L}\boldsymbol{\mathbf{z}}_{l} is a multi-hot encoded version of the input (similar to FlyVec). Note that the energy of the original FlyVec model (see eq.ňú1) is a special case of our approach when ūĚźĖ\boldsymbol{\mathbf{W}} is real and the inputs are preprocessed as in fig.ňú1. Equationňú4 favors learning complex weights for words in one half of the complex plane, since the absolute angle difference |Arg‚čÖ|\lvert\operatorname{Arg}\cdot\rvert is maximized, and its maximum is at ŌÄ\pi. If we replaced the additions in both eqs.ňú4 and 5 with subtractions, the synapses would be learned on the same half-plane as the input vectors ūĚź≥\boldsymbol{\mathbf{z}}. Either option is of no consequence for the model and we choose the additive expression for our experiments. Furthermore, we highlight that the two summands in eq.ňú5 are not of the same scale; the former, |‚ü®ūĚźĖőľ,ūĚź≥l‚ü©H|\lvert\langle\boldsymbol{\mathbf{W}}_{\mu},\boldsymbol{\mathbf{z}}_{l}\rangle_{H}\rvert, is in [0,‚ąě)[0,\infty), whereas the latter, |Arg‚ü®ūĚźĖőľ,ūĚź≥l‚ü©H|\lvert\operatorname{Arg}\langle\boldsymbol{\mathbf{W}}_{\mu},\boldsymbol{\mathbf{z}}_{l}\rangle_{H}\rvert, is in [0,ŌÄ][0,\pi]. To consider this, we experimented with replacing addition (++) by multiplication (‚čÖ\cdot) in eq.ňú4 and eq.ňú5. This however resulted in instability leading to model overfitting (multiple neurons learn the same sequences) and, more prominently, would not constitute a generalization of the original energy presented in eq.ňú1. Nevertheless, in the case of inference, we also test the multiplicative approach.

‚ÄĘ Element-wise multiplication: For every word and neuron, we perform a complex multiplication, which translates to scaling absolutes and subtracting phases. The former indicates the correlation between the input intensity and the neuron‚Äôs output, while the latter gives us a measure of the distance (temporal difference) between the word in neuron ūĚźĖőľ\boldsymbol{\mathbf{W}}_{\mu} and the sentence.

‚ÄĘ Aggregation: Simply adding these complex numbers does not retain positional difference information, since ei‚Äčőł=ei‚Äč2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äčőłe^{i\theta}=e^{i2\pi\theta} for any őł‚ąą‚ĄĚ\theta\in\mathbb{R}. Hence, we instead sum the magnitudes and absolute values of phase differences to capture both the effect of maximum word activation (as in the FlyVec model) and the word position distance.

The former indicates the correlation between the input intensity and the neuron‚Äôs output, while the latter gives us a measure of the distance (temporal difference) between the word in neuron ūĚźĖőľ\boldsymbol{\mathbf{W}}_{\mu} and the sentence.

Aggregation: Simply adding these complex numbers does not retain positional difference information, since ei‚Äčőł=ei‚Äč2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äčőłe^{i\theta}=e^{i2\pi\theta} for any őł‚ąą‚ĄĚ\theta\in\mathbb{R}. Hence, we instead sum the magnitudes and absolute values of phase differences to capture both the effect of maximum word activation (as in the FlyVec model) and the word position distance.

Note that, as depicted in fig.ňú2, we limit the phases in the input ūĚź≥\boldsymbol{\mathbf{z}} to lie in [0,ŌÄ)[0,\pi) to enforce learning phases in ūĚźĖ\boldsymbol{\mathbf{W}} that also only span half of the complex plane. paragraphs: We illustrate the mechanics of the loss presented in eq.ňú4 with a toy example that uses only four KCs (K=4K=4) and ten words (digits) (Nvoc=10N_{\text{voc}}=10) for two sentences (sequences): 1,2,‚Ä¶,91,2,\dotsc,9 and 9,8,‚Ä¶,19,8,\dotsc,1. Both sequences are explicitly learned in the network in the opposite half-plane of the input (that is, with angles in [‚ąíŌÄ;0)[-\pi;0)). The 11-WTA activation (eq.ňú4) successfully creates a sparse representation of the two sequences without interference. This can be observed since the remaining neurons are unchanged and the two sequences are learned (imprinted) in two distinct neurons.

where k‚ąą‚Ąēk\in\mathbb{N}, the hash length, is a hyperparameter. Since the two summands are, as noted earlier, of different scales, we also experiment with a version we call ComplyM that does multiplication (‚čÖ\cdot) instead of addition (++) in eq.ňú6 above.
We evaluate our methods in sentence semantic similarity tasks, as well as in a text-based Reinforcement Learning (RL) environment.

subsections.title: 4.1 Baselines
paragraphs: We focus on FlyVec since it is the architecture we extend. For the sentence similarity tasks, we also compare against BERT embeddings which are contextual representations based on the Transformer architecture. We use the bert-base-uncased weights publicly available on Hugging Face.222https://huggingface.co/google-bert/bert-base-uncased Likewise, we use the publicly available weights of FlyVec. 333https://pypi.org/project/flyvec/ Following the evaluation in [Yuchen et al., 2021], we constrain the tokenizer of BERT to the top 20,000 word indices for a fair comparison against FlyVec and our models.

In the RL evaluation, we compare against a FlyVec model that we pretrain on PubMed (P-FlyVec), and the best performing Transformer-based encoder model in [Winter et al., 2024], denoted as Transformer-MLM. This architecture provides a policy with an auxiliary language modeling objective that is crucial to stabilize Transformer-encoder learning in online RL settings. We use this approach for both P-FlyVec and Comply.

subsections.title: 4.2 Data and model training
paragraphs: We use large text corpora to train the models for our experiments. Sentences are the largest sequence structure that we model with Comply (see sectionňú3.2) and we create batches accordingly. Although the sliding window approach is only relevant for FlyVec (see fig.ňú1), we use it in our implementation only to construct batches of the same shape (phases of the input are independent of this), and guarantee that Comply sees tokens as often as FlyVec. We pretrain our models on two different text corpora depending on the domain of the target task:

For the sake of comparability with [Yuchen et al., 2021], we use the OWC dataset to train Comply. It consists of roughly eight million web documents filtered to be longer than 128 tokens in English, resulting in about 40GB of text files. We preprocess and encode these text data with the FlyVec tokenizer provided in [Yuchen et al., 2021]. It maps every word to a single integer in a vocabulary of 20,000 IDs, resulting in approximately 6.5B tokens. This model is evaluated against the semantic similarity tasks.

We construct a dataset from the PubMed database444https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/download/ keeping only abstracts of articles as in [Gu et al., 2021]. Their model, PubMedBERT, highlights that in-domain abstracts are sufficient to achieve competitive results in biomedical tasks. We filter for abstracts in English and use this tokenizer to yield approximately 6.9B tokens. We pretrain both P-FlyVec and Comply with these data. The resulting models have the same number of parameters and we use them in the RL evaluation.

subsections.title: Open Web Corpus (OWC)[Gokaslan and Cohen, 2019].
paragraphs: For the sake of comparability with [Yuchen et al., 2021], we use the OWC dataset to train Comply. It consists of roughly eight million web documents filtered to be longer than 128 tokens in English, resulting in about 40GB of text files. We preprocess and encode these text data with the FlyVec tokenizer provided in [Yuchen et al., 2021]. It maps every word to a single integer in a vocabulary of 20,000 IDs, resulting in approximately 6.5B tokens. This model is evaluated against the semantic similarity tasks.

subsections.title: PubMed.
paragraphs: We construct a dataset from the PubMed database444https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/download/ keeping only abstracts of articles as in [Gu et al., 2021]. Their model, PubMedBERT, highlights that in-domain abstracts are sufficient to achieve competitive results in biomedical tasks. We filter for abstracts in English and use this tokenizer to yield approximately 6.9B tokens. We pretrain both P-FlyVec and Comply with these data. The resulting models have the same number of parameters and we use them in the RL evaluation.

subsections.title: 4.3 Semantic similarity evaluation
paragraphs: We emphasize that Comply creates representations for sentences. We are interested in evaluating the pretrained embeddings learned in an unsupervised manner without further fine-tuning. Hence, we select the subset of tasks compiled in the Massive Text Embedding Benchmark (MTEB) [Muennighoff et al., 2023] that targets sentence embeddings without additional models (classification heads or clustering algorithms). Furthermore, we focus on tasks comprising English sentences. This results in the majority of the Semantic Textual Similarity (STS), as well as Pair Classification tasks.

The STS tasks are the SemEval workshop tasks STS12-17 in addition to BIOSESS, SICK-R, and STSBenchmark. In these tasks, the similarity between two sentences is assessed based on the embeddings produced by a model. For FlyVec and Comply, we construct their respective contextual feature hashes and for BERT we use the average of all token embeddings. We report Spearman rank correlation based on cosine similarity as in [Reimers et al., 2016].

In the Pair Classification (PC) tasks, a model predicts whether two sentences are duplicates in a binary setting. The metric reported for these PC tasks is the average precision based on cosine similarity. These tasks are SprintDuplicateQuestions, TwitterSemEval2015, and TwitterURLCorpus.

Finally, we include the Words in Context (WiC) [Pilehvar and Camacho-Collados, 2019] task for comparability with the accuracy scores reported in [Yuchen et al., 2021]. Here, the goal is to disambiguate, in a binary sense, whether a specific word appearing in two sentences conveys the same meaning, given their context. We closely follow the evaluation protocol explained in [Yuchen et al., 2021] when evaluating our models.

For all tasks, we conduct a 5-fold cross-validation analysis. Following [Yuchen et al., 2021], we use only one fold to determine the hyperparameters for testing on the remaining four folds. For the MTEB tasks, we optimize the hash length kk. For WiC, we optimize the same hyperparameters as in [Yuchen et al., 2021].

subsections.title: 4.4 Reinforcement Learning (RL) evaluation
paragraphs: In practice, learning policies for RL environments require a significantly large number of environment interactions and training iterations. We argue that P-FlyVec and Comply are computationally efficient, and thus, well-suited for this scenario. RL involves simulation environments phrased as Markov Decision Processes (MDP) that involve a very large number of learning iterations. For our evaluation, we use DDxGym [Winter et al., 2024], which is an RL environment focused on differential diagnosis ‚Äď the process of elimination that doctors employ to determine the diagnoses of patients. This environment generates sequences of text describing the state of a patient. Here, an agent learns a policy to choose diagnosis or treatment actions to cure a patient. The diagnosis actions uncover state information (e.g., symptoms) about the patient, while the treatment actions treat them. Since not all the information about the state (symptoms, severity, and overall health condition) is present in each observation, this is a partially observable MDP (POMDP). The environment is based on a knowledge graph of 111 diseases and an action space of 330 actions. We compare the mean reward and mean episode length of all models in this environment. Both metrics show the level of success to diagnose and treat a simulated patient given a textual representation of their state.

All evaluated models use the auxiliary language modeling objective described in [Winter et al., 2024]. In the case of P-FlyVec and Comply, this objective is based on the energy in eq.ňú1 and eq.ňú4, respectively. For the action and value function prediction, we use a projection layer on the hashes, ignoring gradient computation for ūĚźĖ\boldsymbol{\mathbf{W}}. This means that ūĚźĖ\boldsymbol{\mathbf{W}} is only fine-tuned by the auxiliary language modeling objective. To compare with the results in [Winter et al., 2024], we use IMPALA [Espeholt et al., 2018] to optimize our policy for a maximum of 80M steps. This algorithm is a state-of-the-art actor-critic method to parallelize the optimization of a policy across multiple workers (GPUs).

subsections.title: 4.5 Hyperparameters and implementation
paragraphs: When compared, we use the same number of parameters for FlyVec and Comply in all experiments. Namely, 400 Kenyon cells (K=400K=400) and their respective tokenizers: 20,000-word indices for OWC and 30,522 for PubMed, i.e., Nvoc=20,000N_{\text{voc}}=\numprint{20000} and Nvoc=30,522N_{\text{voc}}=\numprint{30522}, respectively. We follow FlyVec to pretrain our models for 15 epochs with a learning rate of 4√ó10‚ąí44\times 10^{-4}, which is linearly annealed towards 0.

Our models are developed with PyTorch [Paszke et al., 2019]. For multiprocess parallelization and RL, we use Ray [Moritz et al., 2018] and RLlib[Liang et al., 2018]. For the WiC task, we conduct hyperparameter tuning using the hyperopt[Bergstra et al., 2013] scheduler. For training, we use Adam [Kingma and Ba, 2015] as optimizer. The sparse matrix multiplications in our model are mainly indexing operations, which we favor over the sparse555https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/sparse.html abstractions in PyTorch, due to a much lower memory footprint.

For pretraining, we use eight A100 GPUs and a batch size of 0.8 million samples. In contrast, the sentence similarity evaluation on FlyVec and Comply is run on 64 CPU cores to compute all hashes. For BERT we use one V100 GPU and a batch size of 8 in the evaluations. In the RL evaluation, for P-FlyVec and Comply we use four P100 GPUs and the same learning rate as in pretraining. For Transformer-MLM we report the results stated in [Winter et al., 2024].
In the following sections, we present the results of our experiments in both semantic similarity and Reinforcement Learning (RL) tasks.

subsections.title: 5.1 Semantic similarity
paragraphs: Tableňú1 presents our results for the semantic similarity tasks.

The scores show that on seven out of 13 datasets, Comply outperforms all other methods. Except for STS12, Comply consistently outperforms FlyVec by a large margin, signaling that for these tasks the added positional information is beneficial. BERT outperforms on five out of 13 tasks. However, in two of these, TwitterSemEval2015 and STSBenchmark, Comply is very close in second place. FlyVec outperforms in STS12, where Comply follows closely. Similarly, ComplyM outperforms FlyVec and BERT in six tasks, however falls short of Comply on all tasks except for STS13. Nevertheless, these results show that ComplyM is an additional viable method to compute sentence hashes.

It is remarkable that Comply manages to bridge the performance gap and improve on BERT with only 14.56% of the parameters, and that this is achieved solely by incorporating positional information. Note that only unsupervised pretraining is involved and no post-hoc methods of model compression [Xu and McAuley, 2023] are employed. In practice, the latter is the most popular avenue to achieving significantly smaller models with comparable performance.

The evaluation times of FlyVec and Comply on CPUs are significantly smaller than those of BERT on GPU. BIOSSES and STS17 are the only tasks for which BERT shows a comparatively shorter time. We argue that this is solely due to the small number of samples in these tasks. This effect is confirmed with datasets like TwitterURLCorpus, where the number of samples is significantly larger. Here, the efficiency benefits of FlyVec and Comply variants become evident (only ‚Čą7{\approx}7 compared to ‚Čą85{\approx}85 seconds of evaluation time). We argue that the differences in efficiency observed in the evaluation time of FlyVec, Comply, and ComplyM are mainly due to tokenization, hardware optimization, and implementation details. Still, in terms of the hash computation both Comply and ComplyM are mathematically more complex as can be inferred from the times of larger tasks, such as TwitterURLCorpus.

This is reinforced when considering pretraining time. Comply takes approximately 15 minutes per epoch, which is significantly higher than the reported time for FlyVec on the OWC corpus ‚Äď eight minutes on less powerful hardware (8√óA1008\times\text{A100} vs. 3√óV1003\times\text{V100}) [Yuchen et al., 2021]. This difference comes from our choice of implementation. We favor an automatic differentiation framework, while [Yuchen et al., 2021] computes analytically a learning rule to fit the parameters. Even in this setting, our training resources of ‚Čą4{\approx}4 hours on 8√óA1008\times\text{A100} are significantly inferior to the ‚Čą96{\approx}96 hours of pretraining of BERT on 16 TPUs reported in [Kenton and Toutanova, 2019]. When we pretrain P-FlyVec for the RL evaluation with the PubMed corpus with our implementation (optimizing eq.ňú1), we report that P-FlyVec and Comply take roughly 12 and 15 minutes per epoch, respectively, highlighting the added complexity of our energy proposed in eq.ňú4.
Additionally, we survey the relationship between the mean test performance in the MTEB tasks and the chosen hash length kk. We present this in fig.ňú4 comparing FlyVec and Comply.

Notably, the scores of Comply are not only higher, but also more regular and concave, allowing for a hash length kk that is optimal with sparsity in the neighborhood of 150150 KCs. In contrast, FlyVec tends to have an optimal kk either with very low or high values (very high and low sparsity). Minimizing the energy of Comply in eq.ňú4 can be interpreted as memorizing sequences. Hence, we argue that the relationship between the scores and kk is due to these sequences being partially distributed across multiple neurons. Although the energy optimized in FlyVec could accomplish the same (due to the sliding window, and as confirmed with the tasks that perform better with very dense hashes), we argue that presenting the additional positional information explicitly in ‚Ąā\mathbb{C} creates more distinct hashes. We observe these partially distributed sequences in Comply and present a qualitative example in fig.ňú5 of the most activated neuron conditioned on a specific sentence taken from [Yuchen et al., 2021]. This neuron shows contextually relevant short sequences that can be extracted due to the relative position preserved in the phases of the words. Examples of such sequences can be found in the caption.

subsections.title: Task scores.
paragraphs: The scores show that on seven out of 13 datasets, Comply outperforms all other methods. Except for STS12, Comply consistently outperforms FlyVec by a large margin, signaling that for these tasks the added positional information is beneficial. BERT outperforms on five out of 13 tasks. However, in two of these, TwitterSemEval2015 and STSBenchmark, Comply is very close in second place. FlyVec outperforms in STS12, where Comply follows closely. Similarly, ComplyM outperforms FlyVec and BERT in six tasks, however falls short of Comply on all tasks except for STS13. Nevertheless, these results show that ComplyM is an additional viable method to compute sentence hashes.

It is remarkable that Comply manages to bridge the performance gap and improve on BERT with only 14.56% of the parameters, and that this is achieved solely by incorporating positional information. Note that only unsupervised pretraining is involved and no post-hoc methods of model compression [Xu and McAuley, 2023] are employed. In practice, the latter is the most popular avenue to achieving significantly smaller models with comparable performance.

subsections.title: Task evaluation time.
paragraphs: The evaluation times of FlyVec and Comply on CPUs are significantly smaller than those of BERT on GPU. BIOSSES and STS17 are the only tasks for which BERT shows a comparatively shorter time. We argue that this is solely due to the small number of samples in these tasks. This effect is confirmed with datasets like TwitterURLCorpus, where the number of samples is significantly larger. Here, the efficiency benefits of FlyVec and Comply variants become evident (only ‚Čą7{\approx}7 compared to ‚Čą85{\approx}85 seconds of evaluation time). We argue that the differences in efficiency observed in the evaluation time of FlyVec, Comply, and ComplyM are mainly due to tokenization, hardware optimization, and implementation details. Still, in terms of the hash computation both Comply and ComplyM are mathematically more complex as can be inferred from the times of larger tasks, such as TwitterURLCorpus.

This is reinforced when considering pretraining time. Comply takes approximately 15 minutes per epoch, which is significantly higher than the reported time for FlyVec on the OWC corpus ‚Äď eight minutes on less powerful hardware (8√óA1008\times\text{A100} vs. 3√óV1003\times\text{V100}) [Yuchen et al., 2021]. This difference comes from our choice of implementation. We favor an automatic differentiation framework, while [Yuchen et al., 2021] computes analytically a learning rule to fit the parameters. Even in this setting, our training resources of ‚Čą4{\approx}4 hours on 8√óA1008\times\text{A100} are significantly inferior to the ‚Čą96{\approx}96 hours of pretraining of BERT on 16 TPUs reported in [Kenton and Toutanova, 2019]. When we pretrain P-FlyVec for the RL evaluation with the PubMed corpus with our implementation (optimizing eq.ňú1), we report that P-FlyVec and Comply take roughly 12 and 15 minutes per epoch, respectively, highlighting the added complexity of our energy proposed in eq.ňú4.

subsections.title: Hash length.
paragraphs: Additionally, we survey the relationship between the mean test performance in the MTEB tasks and the chosen hash length kk. We present this in fig.ňú4 comparing FlyVec and Comply.

Notably, the scores of Comply are not only higher, but also more regular and concave, allowing for a hash length kk that is optimal with sparsity in the neighborhood of 150150 KCs. In contrast, FlyVec tends to have an optimal kk either with very low or high values (very high and low sparsity). Minimizing the energy of Comply in eq.ňú4 can be interpreted as memorizing sequences. Hence, we argue that the relationship between the scores and kk is due to these sequences being partially distributed across multiple neurons. Although the energy optimized in FlyVec could accomplish the same (due to the sliding window, and as confirmed with the tasks that perform better with very dense hashes), we argue that presenting the additional positional information explicitly in ‚Ąā\mathbb{C} creates more distinct hashes. We observe these partially distributed sequences in Comply and present a qualitative example in fig.ňú5 of the most activated neuron conditioned on a specific sentence taken from [Yuchen et al., 2021]. This neuron shows contextually relevant short sequences that can be extracted due to the relative position preserved in the phases of the words. Examples of such sequences can be found in the caption.

subsections.title: 5.2 Reinforcement Learning (RL)
paragraphs: In fig.ňú6, we present the results of the evaluation on DDxGym [Winter et al., 2024].

Both mean episode reward and mean episode length estimate how well the models learn to solve this environment. For the Transformer baseline both metrics are taken from [Winter et al., 2024]. Comply outperforms the Transformer baseline roughly after 15M environment steps, and it continues to improve until the 80M mark. The Transformer converges to a mean reward of 400 after roughly 10M steps. Since the theoretical maximum for the reward in DDxGym is at about 1200, there is significant room for improvement. The mean episode length shows a similar behavior ‚Äď here a lower value means that the episode ends successfully with the diagnosis both uncovered and treated. While the Transformer converges at around 18 steps per episode, Comply improves until the experiment stops at 80M episodes with around 16 steps per episode. Although P-FlyVec initially reaches a reward of almost 0, it fails to improve further and shortly collapses in performance. This further confirms that capturing positional information significantly improves the performance of Comply, and makes the method more competitive to larger sequence-to-sequence models like Transformers.

We present Comply, an approach to improve learning of sequences by introducing positional information to a biologically-motivated model of the mushroom body of a fruit fly. We achieve this by a novel loss that leverages representations in the complex field ‚Ąā\mathbb{C} and generalizes FlyVec [Yuchen et al., 2021] to learning sentences. Our experiments show that Comply improves sequence representations without adding more parameters. In sentence similarity tasks we significantly bridge the gap between these biologically-inspired models and larger Transformer encoders like BERT. We show that the resource efficiency of our method makes it suitable for challenging settings like Reinforcement Learning (RL), where it outperforms a Transformer baseline. Our analysis expands on the mechanism of the loss we introduce and the interpretability of the learned feature representations.

subsections.title: Tasks and modalities.
paragraphs: We limit our experiments to pretraining our model on large general-purpose corpora. Including the objective of sequence similarity as in sentence Transformers [Reimers and Gurevych, 2019] would expand the comparison to such models. Although our work presents results on the textual modality for comparability with previous work, we believe that our method is applicable to different modalities. Thus a more extensive evaluation of, e.g., different RL environments or time-series tasks is needed.

subsections.title: Loss and model design.
paragraphs: Word repetition is supported in our model in a limited way. Namely, the phases of repeated words are averaged by the aggregation (see eq.ňú4) during training. Improvements in our method could stem from incorporating word repetition as a factor when learning sub-sequences across neurons as we show qualitatively in fig.ňú5. Furthermore, for the sake of comparability, we do not extend our evaluation to larger models (larger KK). Scaling induces problems such as dead neurons, so strategies like the GABA switch as proposed by [Bricken et al., 2023] or similar extensions could be needed. Thus, future work would involve a study on the effect of scale on our approach. Finally, the inherent benefit of adding positional information into a learned representation yields the potential for sequence generation. We do not explore this in our work, but we see making the model generative as a future direction of research.
